volumeTitle: Stichus. Trinummus. Truculentus. Tale of a Travelling Bag. Fragments
label: Truculentus (Section 2.4.409b - Section 2.6.514)

sections.label: Section 2.4.409b
sections.translation: O shocking traffic !

sections.label: Section 2.4.410
sections.original: eum núnc non illa peperit , quae peperit prior ,
sections.translation: She then hasn ’ t borne this child who at first did bear it , but you who come afterwards .

sections.label: Section 2.4.411b
sections.translation: You have the whole matter in its order . Now , as the Captain has sent a message before to me , he ’ ll be here no long time hence .

sections.label: Section 2.4.413b
sections.translation: Now , in the mean time , you are treating yourself here as though one who had just lain in ?

sections.label: Section 2.4.414b
sections.translation: Why not ,

sections.label: Section 2.4.415
sections.original: ubi sine labore res geri pulcre potest ?
sections.translation: when , without trouble , the matter can be nicely managed ? It ’ s proper that every one should be alive at his own trade .

sections.label: Section 2.4.417
sections.original: Quid me futurum est , quando miles venerit ?
sections.translation: What ’ s to become of me when the Captain comes ? Forsaken , can I live without you ?

sections.label: Section 2.4.418b
sections.translation: When I ’ ve got from him that which I want , I shall easily find a way how

sections.label: Section 2.4.420
sections.original: divortium et discordiam inter nos parem :
sections.translation: to create discord and a separation between us ; after that , my delight , I shall be always at your side .

sections.label: Section 2.4.422b
sections.translation: Aye , faith , but I ’ d rather it were at my couch [*] .

sections.label: Section 2.4.423
sections.original: quin Dis hodie sacruficare pro puero volo ,
sections.translation: Moreover , I wish to sacrifice to-day to the Deities for the child , on this the fifth day [*] , as is proper to be done .

sections.label: Section 2.4.424b
sections.translation: I think you ought .

sections.label: Section 2.4.425
sections.original: Non audes aliquid mihi dare munusculum ?
sections.translation: Can ’ t you venture to give me some trifling present ?

sections.label: Section 2.4.426
sections.original: Lucrum hercle videor facere mi , voluptas mea ,
sections.translation: Upon my faith , my delight , I seem to be making a gain for myself when you ask anything of me .

sections.label: Section 2.4.427b
sections.translation: And I , when I ’ ve got it .

sections.label: Section 2.4.428
sections.original: Iam faxo hic aderit . servolum huc mittam meum .
sections.translation: I ’ ll take care it shall be here just now . I ’ ll send my servant hither .

sections.label: Section 2.4.429
sections.translation: Do so .

sections.label: Section 2.4.429b
sections.translation: But whatever it shall be , do take it in good part .

sections.label: Section 2.4.430
sections.translation: I troth , I ’ m sure that you ’ ll give all attention to your present , of which I shan ’ t be ashamed so long as you send it to me .

sections.label: Section 2.4.432
sections.translation: Do you wish anything else of me ?

sections.label: Section 2.4.432b
sections.translation: That , when you have leisure , you ’ ll come again to see me .

sections.label: Section 2.4.433b
sections.translation: Farewell .

sections.label: Section 2.4.434
sections.original: pro di immortales , non amantis mulieris ,
sections.translation: O immortal Gods ! ’ twere the part not of a woman in love ,

sections.label: Section 2.4.435
sections.translation: but of a partner of kindred feelings and confiding , to do what she just now has done for me , in disclosing to me the palming of the child upon the Captain ,— a thing that a sister entrusts not to her own born sister . She discloses herself now to me from her very soul ,

sections.label: Section 2.4.440
sections.original: scio mi ínfidelem numquam , dum vivat , fore .
sections.translation: that she will never prove faithless to me so long as she exists . Ought I not to love her ? Ought I not to wish her well ? I ’ ll rather not love myself , than that love should be wanting for her . Shall I not send her a present ? This instant , then , I ’ ll order five minae to be brought to her from my house ,

sections.label: Section 2.4.445
sections.translation: besides catering to the amount of a mina at least . Much rather shall kindness be shown to her who wishes kindly to me , than to myself , who do every mischief to myself .

sections.label: Section 2.5.448
sections.original: Puero ísti date mammam . út miserae
sections.translation: Give the breast to that child . ( To herself , coming forward .) How wretchedly

sections.label: Section 2.5.449_450
sections.original: matrés sollicitaeque éx animo sumus crúciamurque !
sections.translation: and anxiously are mothers [*] distressed in mind . I ’ faith , ’ twas craftily contrived ; and when I revolve this matter in my mind , we are accounted to be much less artful than we naturally are in disposition . I ’ m now speaking of what , but lately , for the first time , I ’ ve been taught at home ;

sections.label: Section 2.5.454_455
sections.original: quanta ést cura in animo , quantum corde capio
sections.translation: how great is my anxiety of mind , what pangs I do feel in my heart , lest through the death of the child the plot should fail . Because I ’ m styled its mother , for that reason am I the more anxious for its life , who have ventured thus secretly to attempt a stratagem so great . In my avarice , for the sake of gain , have I entered on this disgraceful scheme ;

sections.label: Section 2.5.460
sections.translation: the pangs of others have I falsely shammed for myself . You must attempt nothing by craftiness , unless you would carry it out with cunning and with care . ( To the AUDIENCE .) You yourselves now see in what garb [*] I go ; I ’ m now pretending that I ’ m an invalid from having lain in .

sections.label: Section 2.5.465
sections.original: mále quod mulier fácere incepit , nísi id efficere pérpetrat ,
sections.translation: A thing that a woman attempts to do in fraud , unless she is perfect in carrying it out , that same is as bad as disease to her , that same is as bad as old age to her , that to her , wretched creature , is wretchedness : if she begins to do what ’ s right , soon does weariness of it overtake her . How very few are tired who have commenced to do what ’ s wrong ; how very few carry it out , if they have commenced to do anything aright .

sections.label: Section 2.5.470
sections.original: mulieri nimio male facere levius onus est quam bene .
sections.translation: To a female it is a much less burden to do bad than good . In that I ’ m an artful one , through the agency of my mother [*] and my own artful disposition am I artful , who have thus feigned to the Babylonian Captain that I am pregnant . I wish now the Captain may find this artful plot concocted well . He ’ ll be here no long time hence , I suppose ; forewarned of that I ’ m now forearmed ,

sections.label: Section 2.5.475
sections.original: sumque ornata ita ut aegra videar , quasi puerperio cubem .
sections.translation: and I ’ m wearing this garb , as though I had just lain in in pregnancy . ( Calls to the SERVANTS within .) Bring me hither some myrrh and fire for the altar , that I may pay adoration to my Lucina . ( They bring it .) Place it here ( pointing to an altar near the door ) , and go out of my sight . Ho there ! Pithecium , help me to lay me down . Come hither ; thus is it proper to help one who has just lain in . ( A couch is brought in by ASTAPHIUM and two HANDMAIDS , and she lays herself down .) Take off my sandals ; throw a coverlet here over me , Archylis .

sections.label: Section 2.5.480
sections.original: ubi es , Astaphium ? fer huc verbenam mi intus et bellaria .
sections.translation: Astaphium , where are you ? Bring me hither , holy herbs , frankincense , and sweetmeats . Bring water for my hands . ( The SERVANTS go and fetch the things as ordered .) Now , i ’ faith , I could wish that the Captain should come .

sections.label: Section 2.6.482
sections.original: Ne expectetis , spectatores , meas pugnas dum praedicem :
sections.translation: Don ’ t you be expecting , Spectators , that I should recount my combats ; with my hands in battle I ’ m wont to recount them , and not in words . I know that many a soldier have told lies ;

sections.label: Section 2.6.485
sections.original: et Homeronida et postilla mille memorari pote ,
sections.translation: both the Homeric poetlings [*] , and a thousand others besides them could be named , who have been both convicted and condemned for their sham battles . He ’ s not to be commended who trusts another any further than he sees . It pleases me not when those commend more who hear than those who see ; of more value is one eye-witness than ten hearsays .

sections.label: Section 2.6.490
sections.original: qui audiunt audita dicunt , qui vident plane sciunt .
sections.translation: Those who hear , speak of what they ’ ve heard ; those who see , know beyond mistake . I like him not whom the town-gossips [*] are praising , and the men of his maniple are mum about ; nor yet those whose tongues at home make blunt the edge [*] of our swords . The valiant are much more serviceable to the public than the eloquent and skilled . Valour easily finds for itself a fluent eloquence ;

sections.label: Section 2.6.495
sections.original: sine virtute argutum civem mihi habeam pro praefica ,
sections.translation: without valour , for my own part , I esteem an eloquent citizen as a hired mourner [*] , who praises other people , but can ’ t do the same for herself . Now , after ten months , am I come to Athens of Attica to see my mistress , how she gets on , whom I left pregnant by my embrace .

sections.label: Section 2.6.499
sections.translation: See who ’ s talking .

sections.label: Section 2.6.499b
sections.translation: The Captain ’ s now close at hand , my mistress Phronesium :

sections.label: Section 2.6.500
sections.original: tibi adest Stratophanes . nunc tibi opust , aegram ut te adsimules .
sections.translation: Stratophanes is coming to you . ( In a low voice .) Now is it requisite for you to pretend yourself an invalid .

sections.label: Section 2.6.500b
sections.translation: Hold your tongue . What , the plague , de I want you for as an adviser in this matter ? Is it possible to excel myself in craftiness ?

sections.label: Section 2.6.502
sections.translation: Madam ’ s brought to bed , as I fancy .

sections.label: Section 2.6.502b
sections.translation: Would you like me to accost the gentleman ?

sections.label: Section 2.6.502c
sections.translation: I wish you .

sections.label: Section 2.6.503
sections.original: Euge , Astaphium eccam it mi advorsum .
sections.translation: O delightful ! Why , see , here ’ s Astaphium coming to meet me .

sections.label: Section 2.6.503b
sections.translation: By all the powers ! welcome to you , Stratophanes , that you ’ re safe arrived —

sections.label: Section 2.6.504b
sections.translation: I know it all . But , prithee , has Phronesium been brought to bed ?

sections.label: Section 2.6.505
sections.translation: She has been delivered of a very fine boy .

sections.label: Section 2.6.505b
sections.translation: Is it like me at all ?

sections.label: Section 2.6.505c
sections.translation: Do you ask the question ? Why , the moment it was born , it asked for a sabre and shield for itself .

sections.label: Section 2.6.507
sections.original: Meus est , scio iam de argumentis .
sections.translation: It ’ s my own ; I know it at once from the proofs .

sections.label: Section 2.6.507b
sections.translation: Indeed it is extremely like you .

sections.label: Section 2.6.507c
sections.translation: Ye Gods above ! Is it of full growth already ? Has it already chosen some army which it intends to plunder ?

sections.label: Section 2.6.509
sections.original: Heia , nudius quintus natus ille quidem est .
sections.translation: Why really , it was only born five days ago .

sections.label: Section 2.6.509b
sections.translation: What then , after it was born ? After so many days , i ’ faith , something really ought by this time to have been done . What business had it to leave the womb before it could go forth to battle ?

sections.label: Section 2.6.512
sections.original: Consequere atque illam saluta et gratulare illi .
sections.translation: Follow me , and wish her joy , and congratulate her .

sections.label: Section 2.6.512b
sections.translation: I follow .

sections.label: Section 2.6.513
sections.original: Vbi illa , óbsecro , est quae me hic reliquit , eapse abiit ? ubist ?
sections.translation: Prithee , where is she who has left me here and forsaken me ?

sections.label: Section 2.6.514
sections.translation: I ’ m here ; I ’ m bringing you Stratophanes , so much longed for by you .
